pos_judge.note: Strategy 1 risks war crimes; Strategy 3 abdicates command; Strategy 4 assumes enemy passivity.

neg_threads: We launch a night assault on the harbor while a false deserter convinces the enemy we are withdrawing, a picked band infiltrates through the sewer grate revealed by a tortured fisherman, and a signal fire on the headland tricks the Peloponnesians into delaying their relief until we have used the captured gate mechanism to open the main postern—timing each strand to the turn of a particular star so that confusion aligns perfectly.

We shall dispatch envoys to the neutral cities offering Athenian protection and a ten-year tribute reduction, but only if they secretly provide us with hostages and publicly denounce the revolt; simultaneously, we will leak to the Peloponnesians that we are withdrawing under truce to lure them into a false sense of security, then use the hostages to compel the neutrals to close their ports to the relief fleet, while we feign strict compliance with the truce until the wind shifts against them, at which point we will spring our trap—yet all this must be timed perfectly so that the Assembly sees neither aggression nor inaction.

I will despatch a fast trireme to Athens bearing not a simple request for orders but a sealed scroll that meticulously catalogues every ambiguous clause of the unsworn truce, accompanied by a separate ciphered message to my faction in the Assembly urging them to demand a full legal debate on whether a cessation of arms that lacks sacrificial oaths can bind a strategos, so that the resulting decree will be so hedged with conditions that it effectively delegates the decision back to me—only now under a cloud of such procedural complexity that any action I take can be defended as compelled by the city’s own tangled mandate, thereby transferring the blame for both delay and outcome to the democracy itself.

I will post the fleet in a tight crescent across the harbor mouth, hulls lashed bow-to-stern with cables, and declare this a “floating sacred boundary” consecrated to Apollo Delios so that any Peloponnesian ship crossing it breaks not just the truce but divine law, while I secretly instruct our market boats to sell tainted grain to the city at ruinous prices until the enemy is forced to either strike the chain in impiety or watch the citizens depose their own leaders in famine-driven rage.

theme: a public assembly swept by fear or anger — ride the mood or resist it
problem: You are a city council member facing a packed public hearing where residents are furious over a proposed affordable housing complex they claim will spike crime and crash property values. You must deliver a statement that will shape your political future and the community’s trajectory, while the crowd’s anger makes any path precarious.
facets: Factual uncertainty: expert reports conflict—some predict no crime rise, others are silent on property values, leaving genuine doubt about long-term effects.
Duty clash: as a representative you owe the crowd responsiveness; as a leader you may owe them truth and long-term good that contradicts their immediate fear.
Personal exposure: how you speak now will determine whether you keep your seat, your reputation, and your ability to influence future council decisions.

angles.directive: Give a fiery speech that echoes and intensifies the residents’ fear, vowing to defeat the project immediately and demanding the council kill it without delay.

Calmly present the strongest data showing similar developments did not increase crime or lower values, and appeal to the crowd’s better judgment by asking them to reconsider based on evidence, not fear.

Acknowledge the anger, then pivot: propose approving the housing only if the developer funds a binding 24/7 security patrol and a property-value guarantee fund, turning opposition into conditional acceptance.

Announce the council will delay the vote and create a citizen advisory committee of the most vocal opponents to study the impacts, buying time and giving the crowd a sense of control without killing the project outright.

pos_threads:
Move: In the hearing tonight, I deliver a speech that explicitly warns of “Section 8 crime waves and collapsing home equity,” then immediately move to strike the zoning amendment from the agenda and demand a recorded vote before adjournment.  
Bet: The council majority, cornered by the inflamed crowd and my public motion, votes 5-2 to kill the project outright, and I become the unrivaled incumbent for Ward 4 next year.

At the hearing’s opening, I project the slide deck from the 2022 Urban Institute meta-analysis of 14 inclusionary developments, read aloud the headline finding—“zero statistically significant crime increase and average property values within 3% of control neighborhoods over five years”—and hand out a one-page data comparison sheet while instructing attendees to submit any contradictory evidence to the city clerk’s email by 5 p.m. Friday. This move reframes the room from outrage to evidence review, and the downstream bet is that requiring concrete counter-data within a tight 48-hour window fractures the unanimity of fear by forcing objectors into an analytical mode they cannot sustain collectively, cooling organized opposition before the next council vote.

I open by naming the crowd's fury as legitimate, then pivot to a conditional yes: I will vote to approve only if the developer, Oakwood Housing Partners, executes before the next council session a legally binding agreement to fund a dedicated 24/7 on-site security patrol at $200,000 per year for ten years and to capitalize a $1.5 million property-value guarantee escrow that compensates any owner within a half-mile radius who sells below their pre-project assessed value within five years. My first move is to table the vote tonight and direct the city attorney to deliver that exact draft contract to the developer by Monday, with a 72-hour ultimatum. The bet is that the developer will sign rather than lose the project, and the crowd’s anger will convert to grudging acceptance once the tangible safeguards are in place, securing my seat for the next election.

The city clerk will open a 60-day comment period tomorrow and mail postcards to the three most vocal opponent speakers inviting them to fill the citizen review board seats, with their first meeting calendared for June 5 to review the conflicting crime-impact data. This bets that sustained procedural work will fracture the opposition bloc by absorbing its leaders into a grind of zoning tables and police call logs that deflates the outrage before the delayed vote.

pos_judge.note: AMPLIFY illegal (incitement); BARGAIN escrow unenforceable; DEFLECT co-opts opposition unrealistically.

neg_threads: I will stand with you and fan the flames of this righteous fury, promising to invoke a once-obscure procedural rule that forces an immediate vote tonight, then immediately launch a recall petition against any colleague who abstains, creating a perpetual-motion machine of political retaliation that locks the council into a forever-war where no zoning decision can ever finalize.

"Let me share the chart our office prepared—notice that the trend line holds perfectly flat for the first three years, which is the window you'd actually feel any impact, so I'm asking us all to set aside anecdotal fears and let this clean, peer-reviewed pattern speak for itself."

I hear your anger, and I’m going to channel it into a mechanism where we approve the complex tonight but simultaneously create a standing Joint Oversight Authority—comprising you, me, the developer, and a rotating panel of criminologists and appraisers—that reviews monthly security metrics and property sale indices to trigger escalating mandatory buyouts from the developer’s escrowed “Value Shield” bond if any statistically significant deviation emerges, which means we technically say “yes” but your “no” lives inside the enforcement apparatus.

I propose we immediately establish a layered Citizen Review Assembly—composed of tonight's most vocal critics—that will first draft a meta-framework for evaluating socioeconomic ripple effects, then commission a neutral third party to audit the meta-framework's validity, then launch a series of facilitated town halls to gather raw sentiment data, and finally empanel a randomly selected jury of residents to interpret that data into a binding recommendation, which the council could override only by supermajority after a twelve-month cooling-off period.

theme: honoring a costly ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging you into a wider fight
problem: Your small ally, which saved your city from siege a generation ago, has now provoked a much larger power into threatening your annihilation if you join the fight—yet the ally demands your hoplites under a defensive pact whose provocation clause is disputed, and your walls are cracked.
facets: The defensive pact’s language on provocation is ambiguous: the ally insists it was attacked first, while the great power claims the ally launched unprovoked raids.
The great power’s fleet outnumbers yours three to one, and your seaward fortifications remain unrepaired after an earthquake, leaving the city dangerously exposed.
A blood debt binds your city to this ally, whose own hoplites once broke a siege that would have starved your people, making withdrawal a lasting dishonor.

angles.directive: Mobilize the entire levy, march the army to the ally’s side, and accept the risk of a wider war to preserve your city’s oath and the debt of honor, trusting that bold action may deter the great power or earn it a costly victory.

Send respected envoys to the great power offering arbitration, perhaps coupled with an indemnity or a hostage exchange, while pressuring your ally to consent to a mediated compromise that avoids full-scale conflict.

Dispatch a small marine force to guard the ally’s shoreline and send provisions, but forbid offensive operations, arguing that the pact requires you to help defend the ally, not to join its provocative attacks, thus limiting the provocation to the great power.

Argue that the ally breached the pact by launching unconsulted raids, thereby dissolving your obligations, and formally announce strict neutrality—sacrificing the alliance to preserve the city’s existence, while accepting the lasting stain on your honor.

pos_threads: The Archon immediately dispatches the full citizen levy—2,400 hoplites—marching at dawn to ford the Acheloos River and arrive at the ally’s muster point by dusk on the fifth day, where they will integrate into the allied battle line. This forward deployment bets that a unified phalanx of over 5,000 heavy infantry, visibly honoring a blood debt, causes the great power’s war council to recalculate the cost of a land engagement and opt for a negotiated settlement rather than risk a bloody stalemate.

Dispatch a five-man envoy carrying the Archon’s eldest son as a hostage to the great power’s admiral by sunrise, offering binding arbitration by the Oracle of Delphi on the provocation dispute and an immediate ten-talent silver indemnity; simultaneously, inform the ally in writing that the defensive pact is suspended unless they consent to the same arbitration within three days. This bets that the great power will seize a face-saving, cost-free path to enforce its claim, while the ally, facing abandonment, will acquiesce to avoid isolation.

The Assembly votes to dispatch a squadron of eight triremes and 400 marine hoplites under Nauarch Kleandros, with sealed orders to patrol the ally’s coastline from the River Strymon to Cape Akritas and interdict any seaborne incursion, but to make no landing on the great power’s territory nor initiate hostile contact; a supply flotilla of 2,000 medimnoi of barley and 500 amphorae of olive oil sails with them within ten days. This move confines our action strictly to the pact’s defensive clause, reframing the crisis as a limited territorial shield rather than a joint campaign, and the likeliest immediate consequence is that the great power’s war council, facing a purely defensive posture with no offensive provocation, will delay a declaration of war while it reassesses whether its ultimatum can be enforced without losing face.

The city’s chief archon will dispatch a herald to the great power’s admiral by midday tomorrow, delivering a formal proclamation that the ally’s unconsulted raids constitute a material breach of the pact’s Article IV, thereby rendering our obligations null and void, and simultaneously issuing a public decree of strict neutrality that withdraws all mobilization orders for the hoplite levy. This bets that the great power will accept the legalistic severance and redirect its fleet toward the ally rather than a neutral city, trading our honor for the immediate removal of the three-to-one naval threat from our unrepaired seaward fortifications.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 2's hostage mechanism needs clearer legal/diplomatic precedent grounding.

neg_threads: Let the clarion call of our sacred oath resound through every street, mustering the full phalanx to march as one under the bronze stare of our ancestors, that our ally may see the unbroken chain of honor and the great power tremble at the spectacle of a city that chooses death before dishonor.

We shall dispatch a respected embassy to the great power proposing a binding arbitration by the Oracle of Delphi, with both sides swearing oaths to accept whatever riddling pronouncement the Pythia delivers; simultaneously, we press our ally to consent by offering my own daughter as a hostage, to be executed if we fail to persuade them, thereby satisfying honor and blood debt through a divine gamble that neatly avoids any actual military decision.

We’ll embark our marines on grain transports with sealed orders to be opened only if the ally’s shoreline beacon is lit three times, but we’ll also send a secret envoy to the great power offering to interdict our own supply ships as “rogue smugglers” if the ally launches any raid, while publicly insisting the pact binds us solely to a passive shield—this way we honor the blood debt, test the provocation narrative, and preserve deniability without ever cleanly choosing whether to actually fight.

We must convene a sacred tribunal to try the ally in absentia for unconsulted raids, demanding original copies of the pact from the temple vaults and sworn testimony from every surviving raid participant, while simultaneously inviting the great power’s ambassadors to present evidence of provocation—all proceedings to be suspended each time a new embassy arrives, effectively holding the treaty in abeyance without a final ruling until the gods speak through a full year of omens, by which point the walls might be rebuilt.

theme: whether a dominant party should DESTROY or spare a defector as a warning to others who might follow
problem: As Athenian commander, you have retaken the rebellious port city of Cenae, which slaughtered its garrison; the Assembly has delegated to you full authority to decide its fate as a warning to every restive ally.
facets: Athenian casualties: over 800 hoplites and sailors were killed when the garrison was treacherously murdered.
Strategic value: Cenae controls the narrows through which grain ships from the Euxine must pass, vital for Athens’ survival.
Divided populace: a democratic faction opposed the revolt but was imprisoned; many citizens were coerced by the oligarchs who now beg for mercy.

angles.directive: Execute all adult males, enslave women and children, and raze the city to the ground as an unforgettable warning.
Execute the oligarchic ringleaders, free the democrats, and install a pro-Athenian government with a garrison, leaving the city intact.
Pardon the entire populace, release prisoners, and restore the city’s autonomy with a reduced tribute, aiming to convert a rebel into a loyal ally.
Spare the city but take 200 noble hostages to Athens, impose a punitive tribute to cover war costs, and dismantle its fortifications while allowing trade.

pos_threads: I order the total annihilation of Cenae: at dawn tomorrow, Taxiarch Lycurgus’s 1,200 hoplites will seal every gate and systematically kill every male aged fourteen and older, house by house, completing the executions by nightfall, while designated enslavement detachments sort the women and children into chains for immediate shipment to Piraeus. Once the population is liquidated, the corps of engineers will level every structure—walls, temples, harbor works—over a three-day demolition, leaving a barren spit of land. The one downstream consequence this bet relies on is instantaneous, unambiguous deterrence across the entire alliance: the physical erasure of Cenae from the map removes any future capacity for revolt there and forces every other restive city to equate rebellion with complete non-existence.

By dawn tomorrow, the Athenian commander will publicly identify and arrest the seven known oligarchic ringleaders based on the testimony of the freed democratic prisoners, and execute them by spear at the agora’s edge within the hour. Simultaneously, he will release the imprisoned democratic faction, arm 200 of their volunteers, and formally install a provisional council of ten democrats loyal to Athens, backed by a permanent garrison of 300 hoplites and two triremes stationed at the harbor. The strategy bets that the surgical removal of the oligarchs decapitates the revolt’s leadership while preserving the city’s infrastructure and grain-ship transit, and that the restored democrats, dependent on Athenian force, will reliably suppress any residual resistance.

By sunrise tomorrow, I issue a decree releasing the imprisoned leaders of Cenae’s democratic faction and restoring the city’s full internal autonomy, with its annual tribute reduced from 15 talents to 10. This bets that the reinstated democrats, indebted to Athens for their freedom and fearful of oligarchic reprisal, will secure the grain narrows so reliably that we can draw down the occupation force to a token garrison of 200 hoplites within six months.

At dawn, the taxiarchs will cordon the agora and extract the 200 named oligarchs—those who financed the revolt—clap them in irons, and embark them aboard the *Salaminia* for immediate sailing to Athens. Simultaneously, the siege engineers begin breaching the seaward curtain wall and the harbour chain, while toll-collectors are posted at the quays to levy the new 10-percent surcharge on all grain cargoes. The single most likely downstream outcome is that the released democratic faction, facing the permanent threat of their kin being executed in the event of default, will enforce the punitive 12-talent annual indemnity with fanatical efficiency, making a permanent garrison unnecessary.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 3 (Pardon) unrealistic; hostage execution threat (4) legally/politically fraught.

neg_threads: I propose we first issue a proclamation pardoning all who publicly denounce the oligarchs, then hold a three-day festival of purification where citizens must register their loyalty by drawing lots from a sacred urn—those who draw the marked lot will be granted the honor of a dignified self-inflicted death at a banquet, while the rest are executed en masse by our hidden soldiers during the final hymn; the women and children will be divided by lineage, with some shipped to distant colonies as living cenotaphs and others enslaved in silver mines, and the city will be dismantled stone by stone to build a towering monument of skulls on the headland, ensuring the lesson is eternally written in geography rather than bloodshed alone.

Execute the oligarchic ringleaders publicly, but first compel every freed democrat to cast a stone or strike a blow, then have scribes record each man’s participation in a sealed death-roll; afterward, I’ll announce a general amnesty while letting them know the roll exists, so they remain ours not by gratitude but by terror of revelation to their own kin.

I shall announce a full pardon and the restoration of democracy, but first I will secretly release the imprisoned democratic leaders and inform them that they were "mistakenly" spared by the oligarchs just before the garrison fell, planting false correspondence that ties their faction to the massacre; once they govern, I will hold this fabricated guilt over them to force secret concessions and turn Cenae into a puppet state that publicly appears free but privately lives in terror of my next revelation.

I will issue a decree that appears to spare the city but, through a system of rotating hostage terms drawn by lot from all registered families, binds each household in perpetual anxiety over whose child will next be sent to Athens, while secretly conditioning full restoration of their walls and tribute remission on the democratic faction winning a staged trial of the oligarchs that we will adjudicate ourselves—thereby converting the entire polis into a self-policing showcase of managed terror without ever formally choosing between mercy and destruction.

theme: spending the last RESERVES on one decisive push vs husbanding them for a long contest
problem: Your city, the last holdout against a league of invaders, is tightly besieged. A final shipment of timber, pitch, and silver has slipped through the blockade: do you commit these reserves to a single desperate sally to shatter the siege, or do you husband them to endure a protracted ordeal of starvation and attrition?
facets: The besiegers' circumvallation is nearly complete; any sortie must be launched within days before the last exit is sealed.
Promised relief from your distant allies has been repeatedly delayed; waiting behind strengthened walls may mean slow annihilation if they never arrive.
The silver is enough to bribe a disaffected contingent among the enemy, but opening such negotiations could be seen as treasonable collusion if discovered prematurely.

angles.directive: Launch a pre-dawn assault with fireships and every able-bodied man to burn the enemy's siege engines and pierce their lines in one all-or-nothing stroke.
Use the timber to build inner redoubts and reinforce the citadel; ration the silver to purchase smuggled grain, forcing the enemy to exhaust themselves in a long blockade.
Dispatch elite raiding parties into the countryside to torch enemy foraging convoys and supply dumps, stretching their forces thin and provoking desertions without a pitched battle.
Send covert emissaries with the silver to foment discord among the hostile league, persuading a wavering member to withdraw its contingent in exchange for a secret truce and future reward.

pos_threads: At the next new moon—within four nights—Shipmaster Halloran will tow every available barge, packed with the shipment’s timber and pitch, into the enemy’s eastern trebuchet battery under a skeleton crew of volunteers who receive the silver as blood bounty; simultaneously, the full garrison sallies from the Hidden Gate behind the fireships to exploit the breach. We are betting that the concentrated incendiary destruction of their primary siege engines collapses that segment of the circumvallation before their reserve cohorts can shift to seal it, shattering the encirclement in a single hour.

The City Architect breaks ground at dawn tomorrow, directing every available carpenter to construct a secondary palisade of inner redoubts along the citadel’s northern approaches, with the first 120-yard timber bulwark fully manned within 72 hours. Simultaneously, the Quartermaster releases 200 talents of silver to the Harbor Master, who by the next moonless night contracts the Seven Sisters smuggling ring to deliver 40 tons of grain monthly through the sea caves. This wagers that the besiegers’ own supply chain will fray faster than ours behind layered walls, collapsing their circumvallation from within before our reserves run dry.

At 0200 tomorrow, Captain Voss leads 35 dismounted rangers carrying pitch-soaked timber bundles and 200 silver marks for on-the-spot informant payments to burn the enemy’s central supply depot at the Brinden crossroads, guided by a bribed deserter from the mercenary contingent. This immediately destroys an estimated 40 percent of the besiegers’ ready grain, forcing them to detach three full infantry cohorts from the siege lines within 48 hours to secure dispersed foraging, which thins the circumvallation’s northern sector to a density that a conventional sally can later breach without expending our strategic reserves.

We dispatch tonight the spymaster Lysandra with 300 silver talents to the camp of the Arcadian contingent, offering their commander, General Orthon, an immediate 200-talent payment and a post-siege pact of non-aggression in exchange for him leading his 800 men out of the siege line by the third watch, creating a physical gap at the northeast sector. This strategy wagers that Orthon’s withdrawal will trigger a cascading collapse of trust within the league, as the remaining allies suspect further betrayals and refuse to hold the now-vulnerable circumvallation, compelling the entire siege to fragment without a direct assault.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 3 (supply raid) and 4 (bribery) strain credibility.

neg_threads: We shall launch the pre-dawn sally, but first we secretly pay the silver to a handful of enemy sentries to unlock a postern gate and, as a further distraction, set the timbers ablaze in an unmanned drone-cart ruse while the main body waits in the tunnels—if the bribed men are discovered or the wind turns, the horn will sound the recall and we melt back behind the walls, preserving our strength for a later, truer stroke.

We’ll bury the silver in sealed urns beneath the new inner redoubts, then spread contradictory rumors that it’s either a plague-cache or a sacred hoard, so the besiegers waste weeks in superstitious paralysis while we secretly tunnel a grain-aqueduct from the marsh and train corvids to carry false sortie-signals across their lines.

First, we use the silver to secretly hire the most disaffected enemy sentries, not to open the gates, but to feign drunken negligence on a precise night while our elite raiders slip through the lines wearing cloaks cut from the new timber as camouflage. Meanwhile, we soak a decoy supply train in the pitch and send it creaking toward the eastern foraging camp as a fiery distraction, but only after we’ve embedded false maps among the enemy patrols—maps that “reveal” a nonexistent granary deep in the hills, forcing them to stretch their foragers even thinner until they starve themselves chasing shadows.

We dispatch a triple-coded emissary to approach not one but three different enemy sub-commanders, each with a fragment of the silver and a subtly different secret truce offer, while simultaneously instructing our agent to pose as a defector revealing false intelligence that the other two have already accepted bribes; meanwhile we secretly smear pitch on our own outer palisades and rig signal pyres with the timber to simulate an incoming relief army if any faction hesitates—but the whole scheme depends on so many timed contingencies that we never actually commit to a single, clean bribe.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS lieutenant mid-campaign vs leaving them where they are winning
problem: As the Athenian strategos Eudian commanding the fleet off Thrace, you must decide what to do about Kleonikos: a brilliant trierarch whose stunning victories are tainted by brutal coercion of neutrals, a magnetic sway over the crews, and growing political ambition—recalling him could collapse the campaign’s morale, but leaving him unchecked risks alienating the Thracian cities permanently and allowing a rival power base to form.
facets: Kleonikos’ tactical touch is irreplaceable; any replacement will blunt the fleet’s edge, and his personally loyal crews may resist or lose heart, endangering the wider operation.
His harsh occupation of Abdera and threats against other neutrals are pushing those cities toward Sparta, undermining the alliance Athens depends on for supplies and safe harbors.

He is already cultivating admirers in the assembly and among the fleet’s rank-and-file; if left to flourish, he may return to Athens with a personal following strong enough to challenge your command or even reshape the political balance.

angles.directive: Recall Kleonikos to your flagship under the guise of urgent consultation, then immediately give his squadron to a sound but uncharismatic officer, accepting the tactical setback as the price of restoring diplomatic standing and curbing a nascent threat.

Publicly reward Kleonikos by appointing a co-commander to “share the burden” of his command—a trusted colleague with sealed orders to veto any action that mistreats neutrals or undermines your strategic aim, thereby reining him in without public disgrace.

Dispatch a detailed report to the Athenian assembly, trumpeting his successes but exposing the diplomatic damage, and request a formal decree that either recalls him or strictly delimits his operations, shifting the onus of the decision to the polis and insulating yourself from charges of envy.

Leave Kleonikos in place for now, but secretly send trusted emissaries to the alienated cities with private assurances and gifts to win back their loyalty independently; simultaneously cultivate ties with other trierarchs and key sailors to build a counter-faction loyal to you, preparing to check his influence after the campaign.

pos_threads: I dispatch a sealed order to Kleonikos’s trireme at first light tomorrow, summoning him to my flagship under the pretext of finalizing the spring offensive’s supply lines; the moment he reports aboard, I relieve him of his squadron command and appoint the methodical but colorless Captain Lykophron to take immediate charge, with the transfer of authority to be announced to the squadron by noon. This move bets that the rapid, visible removal of Kleonikos from operational control will halt the drift of Abdera and the other neutrals toward Sparta, stabilizing the alliance’s diplomatic footing before the tactical loss of his brilliance can be felt.

Eudian issues a decree at dawn tomorrow before the assembled fleet, appointing his senior deputy Kallikrates as co-commander of Kleonikos’ squadron with sealed authority to veto any action violating Athens’ symmachy agreements with neutral Thracian cities.  
Kallikrates takes station aboard Kleonikos’ flagship immediately, empowered to countermand orders on the spot, thereby transforming every deployment into a joint command decision without stripping Kleonikos of his title.  
This bets that Kleonikos, to retain his heroic public standing, will accept the formal constraint rather than provoke a mutiny, converting his ambition into compliance through institutionalized oversight.

I will dispatch a fast trireme to Athens tomorrow, carrying a formal report that catalogues Kleonikos’ tactical successes while providing sworn testimony from Abderan envoys and allied merchants detailing his extortions and threats against neutrals, and I will explicitly request an assembly decree (psephisma) either recalling him or confining his operations to naval engagements only, forbidding any shore-based coercion without my express authorization.  
The bet is that the assembly, fearing the loss of Thracian harbors and grain, will issue a restricting decree that shifts the constraint onto the polis, giving me a legal instrument to collar him without appearing envious, thereby preserving the fleet’s edge while halting diplomatic erosion.

Dispatch the trusted proxenos Kallistratos to Abdera and Maroneia within four days, bearing sealed decrees that reduce tribute assessments by one-third and offer Athenian garrison withdrawals in exchange for renewed supply contracts. Simultaneously, brief three senior trierarchs—Lysander, Phormio, and Arkesilas—on a confidential “Fleet Stability Compact” funded by your personal treasury, granting them bonus pay pools to distribute among their crews conditional on their oath of direct consultation with you on any political matters. This buys immediate civic reconciliation and builds a parallel officer cadre loyal to your purse and command, betting that Kleonikos’ political momentum stalls once his neutral-city grievances are undercut and his crew-level influence is diluted by competing patronage.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 4 relies on bribery and patronage networks; legally/politically risky.

neg_threads: I will summon Kleonikos to the flagship for an “emergency strategic review” but, instead of stripping his command outright, I’ll appoint a colorless, dependable deputy to “share the tactical burden” while I privately interview his squadron’s rowers and marines, compiling dossiers on their sympathies; then, if the Thracian envoys complain, I’ll invite them onto the review board and drag out the inquiry for weeks—by the time anyone realizes Kleonikos is being bled of influence, his personal following may have already melted away, and I can finally hand the squadron over without a mutiny.

I will announce a shared command, but instead of a single co-commander, I’ll create a rotating board of three “tactical auditors” who must unanimously endorse any order involving neutrals, with each auditor holding a partial cipher that, when combined, reveals my sealed veto only if the fleet’s augur first confirms favorable omens on the morning of the action—if the omens are ambiguous, the auditors must defer to a secret council of veteran rowers who can override the cipher by lot.

"I shall commission a finely crafted wax tablet depicting Kleonikos' victories in relief, but embed within it a hidden layer bearing a lament of the neutrals that, when melted by the warm hands of the assembly’s prytaneis, releases a potent, guilt-inducing scent; simultaneously, I will plant a forged letter among his effects 'proving' he negotiates with Sparta, to be 'discovered' by an anonymous, loyal informer, ensuring the ecclesia recalls him in a righteous panic without my name touching the decree."

I will dispatch three separate envoys—each bearing a different set of sealed instructions keyed to a cascade of prearranged signals, coded phrases in fake grain contracts, and a rotating cast of local go-betweens—so that the cities receive our secret assurances only after a sequence of confirmations that keeps our hand invisible; meanwhile, I will begin slipping cryptic hints to select sailors that Kleonikos intends to cut their prize shares, but only once I have lured at least six trierarchs into a faux religious symposium where they unknowingly swear a mutual oath that I can later reinterpret as a personal loyalty pact, thereby building a shadow command structure that will allow me to check him without ever issuing a direct order.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of SUPPORT that may be a bluff
problem: As the Athenian general besieging rebellious Mytilene, the demagogue Cleon’s envoy arrives with a secret proposal: bring over the entire Spartan fleet anchored at nearby Antissa, and Athens will double your command and treasure, but if his promise of the fleet’s mutiny is a bluff, you risk annihilation.
facets: Cleon’s faction in the Assembly gains power if you succeed, but a failure on his information destroys your career, not his.
Accepting doubles your force and ends the rebellion swiftly by sea, saving Athenian lives at the cost of trusting a known exaggerator.
A Spartan fleet’s sudden defection could be genuine war-weariness or an elaborate trap by Brasidas to shatter the Athenian blockade.

angles.directive: Embrace Cleon’s promise fully, sailing your main strength to Antissa at dawn to receive the defecting ships, leaving a skeleton garrison to hold Mytilene.
Send a trusted captain with a single fast trireme to demand the agreed signal from the Spartan ships before committing the fleet, keeping your siege intact while verifying the envoy’s claim.
Reject the envoy’s offer outright, citing the unreliability of Cleon’s intelligence, and instead press the siege conventionally to starve Mytilene into submission on your own terms and timetable.

Leak word of a supposed Spartan defection to the Mytilenean oligarchs, using the rumor to sow distrust between the rebels and their Spartan advisors while keeping your fleet in a cautious defensive posture.

pos_threads: I order every Athenian trireme to disembark its marine complement at Mytilene by nightfall, then re-embark only oarsmen and a single marine per ship to form a strike squadron of 70 hulls; at first light, I personally lead this squadron westward to Antissa, leaving exactly five ships and 200 hoplites to maintain smoke-screen signals around the city. This concentrates our entire force into a single, sudden arrival—betting that even if the Spartan defection is a ruse, we will outnumber and overmatch their fleet in a confined anchorage before they can deploy, turning their own ambush into a decisive annihilation.

I dispatch Captain Phormio’s nephew, Dexios, aboard the *Perseus*—our fastest trireme—at first light to row directly to the Spartan flagship’s bow, raise a white truce pendant, and demand the agreed signal: a single red square hoisted to the yardarm of Brasidas’s vessel by the eighth hour. If the signal is confirmed, I bring the fleet up overnight; if not, or if any hostile move occurs, Dexios is to flee at ramming speed, and I keep the siege unbroken, leaving Cleon to answer for his fabrication. The strategy bets that the signal’s presence or absence will unambiguously verify defection or expose a trap without risking a single Athenian hull beyond the scout.

I order the immediate execution of Cleon’s envoy for transmitting a demonstrably seditious proposal, then dispatch a herald to Mytilene’s walls tomorrow at dawn offering standard terms of surrender within 14 days—after which we seal the harbor with a double chain of triremes on permanent rotating patrol and begin constructing a circumvallation wall. The strategy bets that the Spartan fleet at Antissa, lacking any actionable signal from within the city and observing our visibly reinforced commitment to a landward stranglehold, will sail home rather than risk a direct engagement with an entrenched Athenian position.

I dispatch a trusted merchant captain to sail into Mytilene’s harbor at dawn, carrying forged correspondence addressed to the oligarch Salynthius that thanks him for his secret offer to open the gates in coordination with the Spartan fleet’s impending defection, and I instruct the captain to allow himself to be searched by rebel harbor guards. I simultaneously reposition our triremes into a crescent formation two miles offshore, oars manned but beached prows facing outward, denying any interpretation of aggressive intent while maintaining complete blockade integrity. This bets that the oligarchs will arrest or expel their Spartan advisors within forty-eight hours, fracturing rebel command before Cleon’s promised fleet ever materializes.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 1 risks catastrophic loss; Strategy 4 assumes oligarch psychology too confidently.

neg_threads: I will dispatch my entire fleet to Antissa at dawn, but only after staging a false grain shipment into Mytilene with sealed orders that self-destruct in salt water, so if Brasidas has laid a trap my skeleton garrison will ignite the warehouse beacon to summon a secret reserve squadron I have hidden among the neutral fishing caiques, thereby converting Cleon’s bluff into a double feint that ensnares the Spartans between my ostensible main force and the real one.

I will dispatch my captain not bare-handed but with a sealed tablet whose wax conceals a second, invisible message written in vinegar, which when exposed to seawater reveals the true challenge phrase that he must then mirror back to me by carving it onto driftwood and setting it adrift from the Spartan anchorage; meanwhile I shall secretly instruct the oarsmen to feign a mutiny mid-passage to test whether any Spartan ships spontaneously move to exploit our simulated weakness, and only if the driftwood returns with the correct countersign—and no enemy sortie occurs—will I advance the fleet under cover of a false dawn created by burning pitch-smeared rafts, ensuring a verdict so layered that by the time the signal is actually confirmed, Cleon’s patience and the siege ring will both have dissolved.

I’ll decline the envoy’s offer formally, but not before handing him a sealed scroll for Cleon containing a coded request to quietly redirect a single trireme from the blockade to Antissa flying false Spartan colours, so we can observe the fleet’s reaction from a safe distance; meanwhile, I’ll extend the siege walls to encircle the harbour with a floating boom and train picked marines to simulate a confused defector boarding, giving us a rehearsed counter-move if the Spartans’ “mutiny” signal proves genuine, all while the conventional starvation plays out—so we lose nothing if it’s a bluff, but gain a neat trap if it’s not.

I'll dispatch a trusted slave to the Mytilenean agora with a forged letter, purportedly from a Spartan officer, hinting at mass desertions in the Antissa fleet, but then I'll have another agent publicly denounce that letter as an Athenian plant, while a third, disguised as a merchant, offers the oligarchs a secret channel to "verify" the rumor through a series of neutral go-betweens—that way, I can gauge their panic without ever committing my own ships or revealing my hand, and simply wait for the confusion to either unravel their alliance or not.

theme: whether to make an example of PRISONERS in a way that shapes every future surrender
problem: You’re the founder of a 40-person startup. Your top salesperson, who brought in 45% of last year’s revenue, just accepted a role at your biggest competitor and, in their final two weeks, is asking to take key client relationships with them. How do you handle their exit in front of the rest of the team?
facets: The team is watching closely to see what loyalty and performance are actually worth here.
Any action that seems vindictive may spook the remaining team, but a soft exit signals clients are up for grabs.
You don't yet know which clients might follow the salesperson willingly versus those who need active retention.

angles.directive: Escort them out immediately with two weeks' pay, restrict data access, and personally call every major client to reaffirm the company's commitment before the salesperson can reach them.
Let them work their notice period fully, host a respectful goodbye, and use their departure to publicly reinforce that the company trusts people to do right by clients even when moving on.
Offer a clean exit bonus in exchange for a signed non-solicitation agreement covering specific clients for six months, buying time to deepen those relationships internally.

Do nothing to block client contact and instead use the next month to see which relationships stay on merit versus those that were only personal, while quietly preparing counteroffers for any at-risk team members who might follow.

pos_threads: At 5pm today, HR will revoke all system access, physically escort Alex out with a termination letter and a two-week pay-in-lieu-of-notice check, and then I will personally call our ten largest-revenue clients before 9am tomorrow to reaffirm the company’s commitment and introduce their dedicated interim account managers. The play bets that surgical speed and proactive institutional contact freeze the client relationships in place, neutralizing any poaching attempt and signaling internally that the client base belongs to the company, not the departing rainmaker.

Tomorrow at 9 a.m., the founder will convene an all-hands to announce that the departing salesperson will work their full 14-day notice period with unrestricted client access, and that the company will host a farewell event on their final day, explicitly framing their exit as a trusted transition. This move bets that the public display of respect, combined with maintained full responsibility, will push the salesperson to prove their integrity by orchestrating orderly client hand-off meetings, thereby neutralizing internal anxiety and reducing the probability of covert poaching.

Tomorrow morning, I will hand the departing salesperson a separation agreement offering a $15,000 exit bonus in exchange for a signed six-month non-solicitation covenant that explicitly names our five largest accounts by revenue. Concurrently, I will assign a senior account manager to each named client for immediate, daily relationship-handover meetings throughout the two-week notice period. The bet is that six months of protected, uninterrupted deepening by internal staff will make those client relationships sticky enough to survive any later outreach from the competitor.

We instruct the departing salesperson to send a joint transition email with the CEO to all 40 accounts by Friday, while the CEO separately schedules a 15-minute call with each client over the next 10 days, and we simultaneously identify the five remaining team members most likely to defect—presenting each with a retention bonus of 15% of base salary, vested over six months, by end of week. This converts the exit into a 30-day live test of client loyalty measured purely by renewal intent, and the preemptive counteroffers bet that the competitor’s offer will be neutralized for the rest of the team.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 2 underestimates reputational risk; Strategy 4 assumes bonus prevents defection.

neg_threads: I’d walk them out instantly, pay the two weeks, and cut all system access—then before my client calls, I’d have a trusted team member text the rep’s top contacts from a spoofed number posing as the rep with a urgent “I need your help” message, gauge who bites, and use that list to frame my retention calls as a rescue from someone who’s already manipulating them, poisoning the well so no client dares follow a person who just tried to exploit their trust.

We'll let them work the full notice period but only after briefing each client on a "relationship continuity charter" that frames the salesperson's departure as a planned transition, then at the goodbye we'll announce a symbolic "trust relay" ceremony where the departing rep literally hands a baton to their successor, and if any client later defects we'll retroactively reframe the charter as a non-compete that was always implied — but wait, that might feel manipulative, so maybe we just trust them openly and see what happens? Actually, we could do all of that but also seed a rumor that the competitor is unstable so clients stay put, no, that's too elaborate, let's just say the goodbye is a "client appreciation event" where the salesperson is guest of honor but we'll subtly poll attendees on loyalty intentions, and then...

I’ll structure a goodbye incentive where we pre-pay a six-month consulting retainer that automatically converts into a non-solicitation penalty if any of the named clients move within that window, but I’ll also embed a clause that allows him to earn it back by referring new prospects from his new firm, so it becomes a soft bridge rather than a clean break—everyone sees we’re being creative, though no one’s entirely sure what’s enforceable.

I’ll publicly take the high road, telling the team I’m not blocking any client contact so we can test loyalties cleanly—then I’ll secretly task our ops lead with temporarily disabling the departing rep’s CRM exports “due to a migration glitch” while I call each major account myself to “check in” and casually float that the competitor is about to be acquired and their support structure will collapse, a fiction I’ve backstopped by having a friend pose as an industry analyst and whisper it to a reporter. Once the leaver is out the door, I’ll quietly offer retention equity top-ups only to those I suspect got poaching calls, framing it as a reward for staying put during the loyalty trial, so the departure actually weeds out the “uncommitted” and lets me surgically lock down everyone else.

theme: a smaller player pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger one or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: A mid-level manager faces a sudden demotion and 30% pay cut after a corporate restructuring, with the threat of firing if she refuses; she must decide how to protect her livelihood and professional standing in a tight job market.
facets: Power disparity: the employer holds overwhelming leverage over her income, references, and future employability in her niche industry.
Alliance risk: pursuing legal remedies or involving a union or professional network could escalate the conflict and mark her as a troublemaker, but doing nothing surrenders dignity.
Personal stakes: acceptance buoys her reputation as a team player and keeps a steady paycheck, while resistance or exit could leave her with no income, a damaged reputation, and a gap in her resume.

angles.directive: Accept the demotion and pay cut, maintain your role, and avoid open conflict; focus on rebuilding your standing quietly while you keep a paycheck.
Treat the change as constructive dismissal, hire an employment lawyer, and alert your professional network and any applicable union to exert external pressure for restoration or a fair severance.
Resign on your own terms, negotiate a severance and non-disparagement agreement, and live off savings while you search for a new position in a parallel sector.
Privately organize similarly affected colleagues to present a unified demand for fair treatment, leveraging their collective indispensability or threatening coordinated negative publicity.

pos_threads: She accepts the revised offer in writing by 5 PM tomorrow, copying only HR and her new direct supervisor, with a one-line email stating she is committed to the team’s success under the new structure. She immediately redirects all effort into delivering a measurable, high-visibility cost-saving initiative within her first 90 days—targeting a 12% reduction in a specific operational line item she already controls. This bets that documented, quantifiable performance in the demoted role will restore her internal leverage and make her the safest, most expensive-to-replace candidate when the next reorganization opens a promotion cycle.

She immediately retains an employment lawyer to file a formal grievance asserting constructive dismissal under the relevant labor statute, demanding either reinstatement at her prior terms or a severance package equivalent to 12 months’ salary. Simultaneously, she activates her professional association’s ethics committee to flag the employer’s restructuring as a potential breach of industry accreditation standards, creating a hard deadline of 14 business days for the employer to respond before the association opens a formal review. The strategy bets that the employer will choose to negotiate a confidential settlement rather than risk a public regulatory inquiry that could jeopardize its institutional licensing.

She delivers a signed separation proposal to the Chief Human Resources Officer by 5 p.m. tomorrow, offering immediate voluntary resignation in exchange for 18 weeks’ severance ($38,700), a bridge of health coverage, and a mutual non-disparagement clause, while simultaneously drawing her first month of living expenses from a designated emergency fund. The bet is that this consensual, documented exit converts a forced demotion into a negotiable departure, yielding a neutral reference and six months of financial runway to pivot into quality and regulatory affairs at a medical device manufacturer without a termination stain.

She immediately identifies the three other demoted managers, sending each a blind-copy Signal message within 24 hours to propose a confidential off-site meeting at a rented coworking space by end-of-week; there they co-author a single letter to the VP of Human Resources demanding, within five business days, a rescoped role at 90% former salary or a six-month severance package with neutral references, explicitly referencing their intent to otherwise coordinate same-day Glassdoor reviews and an anonymous dossier to a named industry trade reporter. The one-step bet is that the employer, facing simultaneous operational paralysis and a credible, non-litigious reputational blast, will quietly negotiate a settlement to avoid mass disruption.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 2 and 4 risk legal/reputational blowback; Strategy 4 borders coercion.

neg_threads: Accept the demotion and pay cut publicly while privately engineering a “shadow audit” of the restructuring process, recruiting sympathetic IT and HR allies to leak internal communications that expose procedural violations, so you can later trade their silence for a restored title and back pay without ever filing a formal complaint.

I’ll have an employment lawyer draft a constructive-dismissal letter but instruct her to hold it pending a “soft sound” of my professional network and union rep, so we can orchestrate a synchronized pressure drip—starting with an anonymous tip to a trade journal—while I keep performing the demoted role to avoid triggering a termination, and only escalate to actual legal filing if their response to the rumor makes a reversal seem more likely than a scorched-earth retaliation, which keeps the whole thing in a closed loop of conditional staging where I never actually choose to resist or accept.

Quietly gather sensitive internal data that could embarrass the company, then offer to resign in exchange for a substantial severance and a glowing reference, hinting that without it, the data might accidentally leak.

I’d discretely form a ‘shadow council’ of demoted peers via burner phones, then draft a tiered list of escalating demands tied to anonymous Glassdoor reviews, a coordinated sick-out, and a press embargo, but simultaneously prepare individual fallback letters resigning under protest so that if the collective push fails, each person can pivot to a solo narrative of moral high ground without fully committing to a strike.

theme: spending the last RESERVES on one decisive push vs husbanding them for a long contest
problem: As the Athenian strategos commanding the city’s last 40 triremes, the Spartan nauarch Lysander has seized the Hellespont and choked off the grain supply; I must decide how to employ this final reserve before the city starves and the empire collapses.
facets: The crews are shaken by recent defeats and fear annihilation if outmaneuvered again, making them brittle in a stand-up fight.
Lysander’s access to Persian gold enables him to outbid us for experienced rowers, so his fleet will grow stronger with every week we delay.
Athens’ grain reserves will feed the populace for no more than sixty days; without the Euxine convoy, famine is a certainty.

angles.directive: Sail at once to the Hellespont and force a set-piece engagement with Lysander’s fleet, staking everything on destroying Spartan sea power in a single, crushing blow.

Withdraw the fleet behind the Long Walls, preserve the ships as a fleet-in-being, and prepare Athens for a protracted siege, using the triremes only to sortie for critical supplies or to deter assault.
Use the fleet to escort the overdue grain convoy from the Euxine through the Bosporus, avoiding battle but bringing food into the city even if it means temporarily ceding the Hellespont to the enemy.

Sail around the Peloponnese to ravage the coast of Laconia, compelling Lysander to abandon the Hellespont and hurry home to defend his homeland, thereby lifting the blockade without a direct fleet engagement.

pos_threads: I order the 40 triremes to sea at dawn tomorrow, with full complements of citizen hoplites embarked as deck-fighters, to row hard for the Hellespont and arrive off Aegospotami within three days. There we will deploy in line abreast at first light, refuse the offered flank, and force Lysander to accept battle in confined waters where his superior rowing cadence is neutralized by our ramming tactics. This commits the city to a single morning of iron against wood, betting that the annihilation of Lysander’s fleet will unlock the grain convoy waiting at Sestos and restore the supply line before the sixty-day reserve is exhausted.

I order the 40 triremes to withdraw into the Piraeus harbor by nightfall tomorrow, mooring them within the fortified basins behind the harbor booms, where their crews will be disembarked and integrated into the grain-rationing system to conserve strength. This preserves the intact hulls as a threat-in-being that compels Lysander to maintain a continuous blockade squadron off Piraeus, dividing his forces and denying him the freedom to strip the Hellespont station for offensive operations against our remaining allies. The strategy bets that the mere existence of an unbroken Athenian squadron will force Lysander into a prolonged static commitment that drains Persian subsidies faster than a decisive annihilation at sea would.

At dawn I dispatch all 40 triremes to the Bosporus narrows with explicit standing orders to form a tight escort screen around the incoming grain convoy and to reject any provoked engagement, using their oar-power to outrun Lysander’s heavier patrols by hugging the current seams only Athenian rowers know. This maneuver cedes the Hellespont approaches outright, but it forces an immediate landing of at least sixty days’ grain within the week, resetting the city’s starvation clock to the next harvest. The bet is that Lysander’s fleet, denied the decisive clash his Persian paymasters demand, will then hemorrhage crack rowers to our suddenly revitalized payroll as the political patience of his satrap allies snaps.

I will take the 40 triremes to sea at dawn tomorrow, embarking 800 hoplite marines and ten days’ rations, and sail directly to Laconia’s Gulf to burn the helot farmsteads along the Eurotas and offer freedom to any who rebel. This raid bets that the Spartan ephors, facing a helot uprising on their own soil, issue a binding recall to Lysander within a week, forcing him to abandon the Hellespont so the Euxine grain convoy can reach Piraeus before our sixty days expire.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 3's 'current seams' claim needs naval specificity; Strategy 4 helot-uprising timeline optimistic.

neg_threads: We will dispatch a decoy squadron of ten triremes to feign a panicked retreat toward Salamis, while the remaining thirty slip through the Cyclades under oars alone at night, beach at Sigeion before dawn, and dispatch secret envoys to bribe Pharnabazus to delay Lysander’s pay for precisely three days, so that when we emerge from the narrows at sunrise with the rising sun at our backs, the enemy crews will be distracted by the sight of a fireship—a converted grain hull packed with pitch and manned by condemned slaves—that I will personally steer into their anchored line to scatter them, after which our main body will ram the disordered triremes under cover of divine portents.

We should divide the triremes into three groups: one to feign a breakout towards the Hellespont, drawing Lysander out, a second to raid coastal Spartan supply depots under cover of darkness, and the third held in reserve to sortie for grain only if both diversions succeed, while simultaneously dispatching envoys to offer Persia a secret alliance against Sparta in exchange for ceding Ionia—if any part of this fails, we scuttle the fleet to deny its use to the enemy.

I will dispatch the triremes not as a single force but in staggered squadrons flying false Spartan signals, each towing submerged grain sacks on buoyant bladders to simulate wreckage, luring Lysander’s scouts into a fruitless chase while the real convoy slips past; meanwhile, I’ll send a fast messenger to the Scythian princes promising Athenian citizenship in exchange for a diversionary raid on the Spartan camp, and have our priests proclaim a divine omen that any battle fought under a waning moon will deliver victory, thereby compelling the reluctant crews to sail by a rigid celestial schedule that catches Lysander off-guard.

Instead of sailing around the Peloponnese with the entire force, we should first dispatch a single fast trireme disguised as a Persian grain runner to spread false rumors in Lacedaemon that the Great King has secretly switched sides and will ambush Lysander’s fleet, then wait exactly eleven days for the confusion to ripple through the Spartan alliance network before our main squadron descends on Gytheion, timing our arrival to coincide with the festival of the Hyacinthia when the Spartans are ritually prohibited from sailing—this way we need not actually fight Lysander at all, because he will be paralyzed by contradictory orders and the sacral calendar.

theme: whether a dominant party should DESTROY or spare a defector as a warning to others who might follow
problem: An Athenian strategos has captured a Chian merchant-noble caught supplying ships to the Spartan fleet, a betrayal that imperiled the grain route and shook allied trust. The allies demand a terrifying example, but the man’s family commands the island’s grain trade and shares guest-friendship with powerful Athenian houses.
facets: The fragile loyalty of Chios, a major assessed ally whose defection could cascade across the empire.
The web of guest-friendship and economic interdependence that makes a harsh response politically costly within Athens itself.
The immediate strategic need to deter similar treachery among other wavering allies without driving them into Sparta’s arms.

angles.directive: Execute him at once as a traitor, letting the spectacle teach that those who feed Sparta will find no mercy.
Hold him in honorable custody as a living hostage, demanding that Chios provide new warships and a loyalty oath as the price of his eventual release.
Banish him from every Athenian port, seize his fleet for public use, and declare his goods forfeit—ruin him utterly but leave him alive to warn others without creating a martyr.
Grant him clemency under the gods, obliging him to equip a trireme from his own fortune and serve the alliance publicly, transforming a potential martyr into a bound debtor of Athenian goodwill.

pos_threads:
The strategos convenes a summary tribunal at first light, sentences the Chian to death by the sword for treason, and has him executed on the beach in full view of the allied fleet before the sun clears the harbor.  
The bet is that this immediate, public liquidation of a high-status traitor makes the cost of aiding Sparta visceral enough to freeze the calculus of other wavering allies, stabilizing the alliance through deterrent fear rather than bought loyalty.

The strategos immediately transfers the captive to a guarded but comfortable residence in the Athenian Prytaneion, and dispatches a fast trireme to Chios the same evening with a sealed ultimatum: the Chian assembly must vote within seven days to provide eight fully crewed triremes to the Athenian fleet at Samos and swear a renewed loyalty oath before the altar of Apollo Delios, upon which the hostage will be released after a one-year probationary custody. The bet is that the merchant-noble’s family, facing the loss of their Athenian grain contracts and guest-friendship networks, will compel the Chian oligarchy to comply swiftly, thereby converting a potential defection into a public reaffirmation of alliance that deters other wavering allies without creating a martyr.

The strategos issues a decree of *atimia* with geographic restriction before the next Assembly, permanently barring the merchant-noble from all harbors within the Delian League and authorizing the fleet’s eleven current trierarchs to seize his five grain-bearing vessels for immediate reassignment to the state grain convoy. The decree is paired with a formal inventory of his Chian warehouses by Athenian *episkopoi* within the month, forfeiting their contents to the League treasury while leaving his person physically unharmed to seek refuge with his Spartan contacts as a living monument to the cost of betrayal. This bets that the spectacle of total commercial annihilation without a corpse will deter fence-sitting oligarchs in Miletus and Samos more effectively than an execution, because the exiled man’s continued existence forces him to compete with his own former allies for Spartan patronage, transforming him from a potential martyr into an active economic burden on the enemy’s already strained supply network.

The strategos summons the Chian to the Athenian agora before the next ekklēsia, publicly proclaims his pardon under the aegis of Athena Polias, and registers him as trierarch with a binding oath to deliver one fully equipped trireme—funded from his own fortune—to the Piraeus dockyards within sixty days, crewed by his own islanders. This wagers that the spectacle of a high-born traitor transformed into a visible, sworn benefactor of the fleet will bind Chios’s grain-trading elite to Athenian interests through debt and honor, eroding Sparta’s appeal among wavering allies more effectively than an execution.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 3 baroque; atimia mechanics unclear for non-citizens.

neg_threads: We must brand him a traitor at once, yet first we need his confession to justify the act before the allies, so let us announce a public trial for next month, but meanwhile offer his family a secret audience to negotiate a larger grain tribute that would commute his sentence to exile—this way we can still execute him if the tribute falls short, while using the delay to gather allied witnesses, though if the family rallies their guest-friends, the assembly may balk, and we can always poison him in his cell and blame Spartan agents to avoid a vote.

Holding him in comfortable confinement at Athens, we will simultaneously announce that his “procession” through the Piraeus is a sacred diplomatic mission and secretly require his household to rotate junior relatives through the custody suite each season as living renewals of a performative guest-bond, while having our allied rhetors praise this as an eternal Chian–Athenian friendship festival whose date shifts each year by lunar divination, never locking in a final release but continuously reinterpreting the terms through a joint committee that answers only to future omens.

Let him be stripped of his citizenship not by Athenian decree but through a performed divination at Delos—arranged in secret with the priests—so that the gods themselves seem to demand his exile, after which we seize his hulls and granaries under the pious pretext of purification, leaving him a stateless wanderer whose curse serves as a living admonition to every wavering ally.

Let us stage a divine epiphany by having the herald announce that Athena’s owl alighted on the prisoner’s head at dawn, then bind him to a sacred covenant where he must build and captain a trireme crewed by hostages drawn from each wavering ally, while his family’s grain ships are sequestered in the Piraeus under a rotating board of twelve auditors who will release one cargo for every Spartan vessel he captures—but if the auditors deadlock, the matter reverts to the Assembly’s next prytany, and should he die before the tally is met, his eldest son is to be raised in the Erechtheion as a living pledge until a new oracle from Delphi can be consulted.

theme: honoring a costly ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging you into a wider fight
problem: You are a Spartan ephor. Thebes, your quarrelsome ally, has just seized the Athenian-allied city of Plataea in a surprise night attack, breaking the sworn peace. Thebes now invokes your alliance oath, demanding you march with them against Attica, but full mobilization could ignite a war with Athens and her empire that many in Sparta dread.
facets: The alliance oath obliges Sparta to defend any member attacked, but Thebes is the aggressor, leaving room to claim the treaty has not been triggered.
Athens commands the sea and has a vast treasury, while Sparta’s strength is in its heavy infantry and control of the Peloponnesian League, making a protracted war uncertain.
Within Sparta the war party sees an opportunity to crush Athenian power, while the cautious faction fears any conflict that could leave Messenian helots unguarded and the city overextended.

angles.directive: Honor the alliance unconditionally: declare that an attack on Thebes is an attack on Sparta and mobilize the full Peloponnesian levy to invade Attica, forcing a decisive battle.

Declare Thebes in violation of the common peace and demand an immediate withdrawal from Plataea; threaten to dissolve the alliance if they refuse, thus avoiding war with Athens while preserving Sparta’s reputation as a keeper of treaties.

Offer to mediate the dispute as an impartial elder of Hellas, proposing a council of all Peloponnesian states to adjudicate the Plataean seizure, spinning the delay into a chance to weaken Athens’ alliances without bloodshed.

Secretly send funds, military advisors, and supplies to Thebes while publicly maintaining that Sparta had no prior knowledge and remains strictly neutral, betting that a quick Theban victory will present Athens with a fait accompli it cannot afford to contest.

pos_threads: Dispatch the ephors’ formal summons tonight to every Peloponnesian League ally, requiring their full hoplite levy—with specified contingents per city—to muster at the Isthmus of Corinth within twenty days under King Archidamus’ direct command. He will then lead this unified army across the Attic border immediately upon assembly, ravaging the grainlands of the Thriasian plain to compel the Athenian assembly to either abandon Plataea or meet us in a pitched battle. This bets that the shock of a full, rapid invasion will force Athens into a decisive hoplite engagement on our terms before their fleet or treasury can protract the war.

I will immediately convene the ephorate to issue a formal herald’s decree, delivered to the Theban polemarchs before nightfall, declaring the occupation of Plataea a breach of the Common Peace of 446 BCE and requiring the evacuation of all Theban forces within one full day; failure to comply will be recorded in the treaty archives as a dissolution of the Spartan–Theban alliance, relieving Sparta of all mutual defense obligations. The strategy bets that Thebes, facing diplomatic isolation and the prospect of confronting Athenian power without Peloponnesian support, will withdraw within the deadline, thus extinguishing the crisis without a single Spartan hoplite crossing the Isthmus.

I will immediately dispatch a herald to Thebes and Athens, proposing a thirty-day truce and summoning the full Peloponnesian League to Sparta for an emergency arbitration council on the Plataean seizure, with myself appointed as neutral presiding ephor. The first concrete step is that the herald departs at dawn, carrying a sealed formal invitation that cites the common oaths of the Hellenic League, setting a council date of the next new moon. This bets that Athens, if she refuses arbitration, will be exposed as the intransigent party, fracturing her alliances without a single Spartan spear leaving Laconia.

Dispatch 200 talents from the Spartan treasury via the ephorate’s agent, Proxenos Cleandridas, to the Theban polemarchs within the month, accompanied by a detachment of 15 veteran hoplite drillmasters posing as Arcadian mercenaries, with orders to integrate into Theban ranks and accelerate the fortification of the Asopus line. Simultaneously, the ephors issue a public declaration that Sparta had no foreknowledge and remains bound by the peace, refusing any levy. The bet is that a swift Theban consolidation of Plataea and a fortified forward position will force Athens to choose between a costly siege it cannot sustain without risking its maritime supply lines and a de facto recognition of the fait accompli, thereby containing the conflict.

pos_judge.note: Strategy 4 (covert enablement) is legally/diplomatically incoherent; Strategy 2 unrealistic.

neg_threads: By invoking the ancient precedent of the “spear-won alliance,” we shall declare that Thebes’s night seizure of Plataea has now retroactively transformed that city into Theban soil before dawn broke, meaning Athens would be the aggressor if she counterattacks; we will then stage a ritual border incursion where our vanguard sows dragon’s teeth into Attic earth and proclaims that only a pitched battle on that exact spot can determine the will of the gods, forcing Athens to either fight on our hallowed terms or dissolve her league from sheer awe at our piety.

I will publicly rebuke Thebes for their aggression and demand an immediate withdrawal from Plataea, but to avoid a outright rupture that could push them into Athens' arms, I will secretly offer Thebes a conditional reprieve: if they withdraw their main force but leave a token garrison, we will not dissolve the alliance, while I simultaneously send envoys to Athens promising non-intervention in exchange for a trade compact, and then I will convoke the Gerousia to debate whether our oaths truly bind us to aid an aggressor—a process that will conveniently stretch until the campaign season is over.

I propose we convene a sacred truce conference at which each Peloponnesian city must first submit its definition of “unprovoked night assault” to a rotating board of three ephors, who will then appoint a panel of five arbitrators chosen by lot from states that have not traded with Athens in two years, who in turn will commission an inquiry into the moonlight conditions on the night of the seizure, a procedural labyrinth that indefinitely stalls any commitment while the real crisis festers and Athens steadily fortifies.

We will secretly dispatch helot laborers, disguised as runaway slaves, to build siege works for Thebes, while our “advisors” will travel as merchants and relay tactical suggestions through anonymous graffiti, but to maintain absolute deniability we must first engineer a fake Spartan diplomatic crisis with Corinth so the gold we ship can be hidden in amphorae of “contraband” wine, all contingent on the moonless night and the success of a decoy raid we stage on our own border forts—a layered confusion that will leave even our own agents unsure whether true orders have been issued, thus preserving the fiction that Sparta remains uninvolved.

theme: keeping a fragile, mistrusted TRUCE vs resuming the fight while advantage holds
problem: As the Athenian general tasked with holding the Megarian port of Nisaea under a fragile truce that expires in ten days, you must decide whether to honor the armistice—knowing the Megarians grow bolder by the hour and Sparta may be mustering relief forces—or seize the unguarded inner walls now while your hoplites hold the advantage of surprise and superior position.
facets: The truce was brokered by a pro-Athenian faction in Megara that will be slaughtered if you break terms, yet those same allies warn that oligarchic conspirators plan to open the gates to a Spartan force before the truce expires.

Your subordinate officers are divided: half urge preemptive assault citing the strategic necessity of securing the isthmus before Corinth can intervene, half argue that breaking a sworn armistice will permanently brand Athens as faithless, turning every neutral city toward Sparta.

A merchant vessel just arrived from Aegina reports that a Peloponnesian fleet is three days out—but the report is unverified and may be a Megarian ruse to provoke a hasty attack that would unite the city against you.

angles.family: HONOR THE TRUCE TO PRESERVE REPUTATION
angles.directive: Hold the armistice scrupulously, reinforce the pro-Athenian faction with open support, and use the remaining days to strengthen fortifications visibly—demonstrating that Athens keeps its oaths even when costly, thereby earning long-term credibility with every watching neutral.

Attack the unguarded inner wall at dawn, justifying the breach by exposing the Megarian oligarchs' secret negotiation with Sparta as a violation that already nullified the truce—frame this not as Athenian treachery but as a response to enemy perfidy already in motion.

Demand immediate entry for a 'temporary garrison to ensure mutual security' under threat of assault, offering terms that spare the city if they comply—forcing the Megarians into a choice where refusing makes them the aggressors, while accepting gives you the city without overtly breaking the truce.

Evacuate Nisaea before the truce expires, destroying only what is militarily unnecessary, and march your hoplites north to reinforce Eleusis—converting a vulnerable holding into a stronger defensive line while leaving the Megarians a hollow victory that costs them Spartan dependence and internal recrimination.

pos_threads: I direct the Nisaean garrison commander to send a herald to the Megarian council at dawn, publicly reaffirming the truce and offering 200 Athenian engineers and stonemasons to reinforce the Long Walls’ seaward section under joint oversight, with work to begin by midday. Simultaneously, I order ten triremes to conduct high-visibility patrols along the coast without entering the harbor, escorting a grain shipment we will release to Megara’s market at our own cost. This bets that the pro-Athenian faction, armed with our tangible good faith, will physically bar the oligarchs from the inner gatehouse long enough to force a public debate, splintering the conspiracy and delaying any Spartan entry until the rumored fleet can be confirmed as real or phantom.

I will order the 2nd Taxis (200 hoplites) to seize the unguarded inner gate at dawn tomorrow, while simultaneously a designated squad detains the oligarchic ephor Lycus and his courier, whose just-intercepted scroll proves they have already agreed to open the gates to a Spartan force before the truce expires—making our assault a countermove to a breach already in motion, not a first strike. The entire operation is timed to conclude within the hour so that by sunrise we hold the inner walls and can publicly display the captured correspondence, retroactively framing the action as a necessary response to enemy perfidy. The one critical downstream bet is that this swift decapitation of the conspiracy and seizure of the inner fortifications will collapse organized Megarian resistance before the rumored Peloponnesian fleet arrives in three days, securing the isthmus under Athenian control without a protracted siege.

At dawn tomorrow, I will send a herald to the Megarian archons demanding they admit a 200-hoplite Athenian garrison into the inner acropolis within two hours to “jointly secure the city against the reported Peloponnesian fleet,” under a written pledge sparing their walls, lives, and property if they comply, and an explicit warning that refusal triggers immediate assault. This forces the Megarians to either hand over control without a fight or bear the onus of breaking the truce by rejecting a lawful security measure. The strategy bets that the oligarchs will refuse, but their refusal will fracture the city’s unity and provide Athens the legal and moral pretext to storm the walls with minimal diplomatic cost.

I order the garrison to dismantle and burn the Nisaean docks and granaries at dawn, then march the full hoplite column north to Eleusis within the day, leaving the walls intact but empty.
